content.text: What it is

Target-state org design for agent delivery is the future-state organizational structure and role taxonomy that an enterprise adopts to industrialize agentic AI delivery at scale. It answers: who does what, how are teams organized, what does the reporting structure look like, and how do central AI functions relate to business-unit delivery teams. Unlike a startup where one team does everything, an enterprise agent delivery organization separates concerns across three verticals — AI platform, AI governance, and agent delivery squads — each with distinct accountabilities, skill profiles, and operational rhythms.
Why it matters

Agentic AI programs fail organizationally more often than they fail technically. The failure mode is structural: data scientists embedded in business units build agents without platform support, creating fragile one-off solutions; or a centralized platform team builds infrastructure that business units won't use because they weren't involved in requirement definition; or governance is added as an afterthought and creates a slow-down without adding proportional risk reduction. The target-state org design prevents these failure modes by establishing the right team topology from the outset, defining clear interfaces between functions, and ensuring governance is embedded in the delivery process rather than bolted on at the end.
Benefits
Eliminates ambiguity about who builds infrastructure vs. who builds agents vs. who governs deployment — preventing turf conflicts that block delivery.
Enables specialist depth: platform engineers focus on MLOps and infrastructure, not agent product logic; agent engineers focus on capability development, not infrastructure.
Creates a talent model with clear career paths: progression from agent engineer to senior agent engineer to technical lead is legible and recruiteable.
Allows delivery velocity to scale without scaling governance overhead, because platform and governance are shared services consumed by multiple squads.
Provides a reference structure for headcount planning and budget justification to the CFO and CHRO.
Trade-offs and limitations
A three-vertical design requires sufficient headcount to staff each vertical meaningfully — a team of 8 total cannot maintain this separation without role overload.
Separation of platform from delivery creates interface friction: delivery squads need to wait on platform team to build features, creating a potential bottleneck.
The BU AI Lead role (embedded liaison) is difficult to hire and retain — it requires both deep AI knowledge and strong business domain expertise, a rare combination.
Target-state design takes 12–24 months to reach from a starting point of scattered AI capabilities, requiring sustained organizational commitment through leadership transitions.
How it works

The target-state design rests on separating three fundamentally different concerns. The AI Platform Team is responsible for the infrastructure and shared tooling layer: the compute environment, MLOps pipelines, LLM gateway, vector stores, evaluation harnesses, and monitoring infrastructure. This team has a platform engineering mindset: their customers are internal teams, their output is APIs and services, and their success metric is platform adoption and reliability.

The AI CoE / Governance function is responsible for standards, policies, risk management, and enablement. It does not build agents or platform infrastructure directly. Instead, it defines the standards that delivery squads and the platform team must meet, reviews high-risk deployments, runs the training program, and maintains the pattern library. Its success metric is governance coverage, training completion, and reduction in AI incidents.

Agent Delivery Squads are cross-functional pods that combine all the skills needed to take an agent use case from requirements through production: a product owner who defines the use case and measures value, agent engineers who build the agentic workflows, domain experts who provide the subject-matter knowledge the agent needs, and an embedded BU AI Lead who bridges the squad to the business unit and to the central CoE. Each squad operates autonomously within the guardrails set by the platform and governance functions.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Target-state org design showing CAIO leading AI Platform, CoE, and Agent Delivery Squads with shared services
attrs.title: Target-State Org Design for Agent Delivery

content.text: The three-vertical structure under the CAIO reflects the separation of platform, governance, and delivery concerns. Shared services (data platform, security, legal, finance) sit below all three verticals, providing inputs without being managed by the CAIO. The design intentionally keeps the CoE as a standards and enablement body — not a delivery org — which prevents it from becoming a bottleneck and keeps delivery velocity in the squads.
When to use it

This target-state design is appropriate for organizations that have committed to AI as a strategic capability and expect to run 5+ agent use cases in parallel. Smaller programs can operate with a merged platform+delivery team and a lighter governance overlay. The three-vertical design becomes necessary when: platform needs start diverging from delivery needs, when governance overhead is growing unsustainably, or when a second business unit is joining the AI program and needs a defined interface to connect to.
How to implement
Conduct a current-state org audit: map where AI work is happening today, who is doing it, and what reporting lines they sit under.
Define the target-state role catalog: for each of the three verticals, specify the roles, required skills, seniority levels, and team sizes needed for the expected program scale.
Identify the gaps: compare current-state capabilities to target-state requirements to determine which roles need to be created, retrained, or recruited.
Design the transition path: org transitions of this scale typically require 3–4 phases over 12–24 months; plan the sequence so delivery is not disrupted during restructuring.
Establish the CAIO role before restructuring the teams that report to it — the CAIO is the organizing authority that makes the three-vertical design coherent.
Define the operating interfaces between verticals: service catalog (platform to delivery), intake process (delivery to CoE), feedback loops (delivery learnings back to platform and CoE).
Examples

# Target-state headcount plan for mid-sized enterprise AI program
# Assumption: 3-5 active agent squads, 2 BUs engaged

ai_platform_team:
  total_headcount: 8
  roles:
    - title: Platform Engineering Lead
      count: 1
      level: Staff Engineer
      skills: [MLOps, Kubernetes, distributed systems]
    - title: ML Engineer
      count: 3
      level: Senior
      skills: [Python, PyTorch, model serving, fine-tuning]
    - title: Data Engineer
      count: 2
      level: Senior
      skills: [Spark, dbt, vector databases, data pipelines]
    - title: DevSecOps Engineer
      count: 1
      level: Senior
      skills: [Terraform, security scanning, SAST/DAST, CI/CD]
    - title: Cloud Architect
      count: 1
      level: Principal
      skills: [AWS/Azure/GCP, cost optimization, HA design]

ai_coe:
  total_headcount: 5
  roles:
    - title: CoE Director
      count: 1
      level: Director
      skills: [AI strategy, program management, stakeholder management]
    - title: Responsible AI Lead
      count: 1
      level: Senior Manager
      skills: [bias evaluation, fairness, AI policy, risk management]
    - title: AI Solution Architect
      count: 2
      level: Staff
      skills: [agent architecture, LLM evaluation, system design]
    - title: Enablement Lead
      count: 1
      level: Senior
      skills: [instructional design, technical writing, training delivery]

agent_delivery_squads:
  squads: 3
  per_squad_headcount: 6
  per_squad_roles:
    - title: Product Owner
      count: 1
    - title: Agent Engineer
      count: 2
    - title: Prompt Engineer / AI Quality Lead
      count: 1
    - title: Domain Expert (from BU)
      count: 1
    - title: BU AI Lead (embedded, 50% allocation)
      count: 1

total_program_headcount: 31  # excluding shared services

# Org design analysis: identify current vs. target capability gaps

current_state = {
    "data_science": 4,       # PhD/MS in data science, model building
    "ml_engineers": 2,       # productionizing models
    "software_engineers": 6, # general SWE, not ML-specialized
    "data_analysts": 5,      # BI-focused, SQL and dashboards
    "it_infra": 3,           # not MLOps-specialized
    "pm": 2                  # general product managers
}

target_state = {
    # AI Platform Team
    "ml_engineers": 3,
    "data_engineers": 2,
    "devsecops": 1,
    "cloud_architect": 1,
    # CoE
    "coe_director": 1,
    "rai_lead": 1,
    "ai_solution_architects": 2,
    "enablement_lead": 1,
    # Per-squad (x3)
    "product_owners": 3,
    "agent_engineers": 6,   # 2 per squad
    "prompt_qa_engineers": 3,
    "bu_ai_leads": 3        # 1 per squad, from business
}

# Calculate gap
gaps = []
for role, count in target_state.items():
    current = current_state.get(role, 0)
    if count > current:
        gaps.append({"role": role, "gap": count - current, "action": "hire/retrain"})

for gap in sorted(gaps, key=lambda x: -x["gap"]):
    print(f"{gap['role']}: need {gap['gap']} more")
Real-world example

A global logistics company with B in revenue launched an AI program in 2022 with a team of 6 data scientists centralized under IT. By mid-2023 they had three different business units (freight, customs, last-mile) independently hiring AI engineers, resulting in duplicated vector store investments, inconsistent model evaluation practices, and no shared platform. The CAIO conducted a six-month org design exercise, resulting in a transition plan to the three-vertical target state.

Phase 1 (months 1–6) established the AI Platform Team by consolidating the three independent infrastructure efforts into a single team under a new Platform Engineering Lead. Phase 2 (months 7–12) stood up the CoE with a CoE Director hired externally and a Responsible AI Lead promoted from the existing data science team. Phase 3 (months 13–18) reorganized delivery into three cross-functional squads aligned to the three BU programs.

Eighteen months after starting the transition, infrastructure cost per agent deployment dropped 41% due to platform consolidation, and the time from approved use case to production-ready agent decreased from 14 weeks to 9 weeks due to the squad model and reusable platform services. The org design paid for itself in under 12 months.
Common mistakes
Designing the CoE as a delivery organization rather than a standards and enablement body — this creates bottlenecks and prevents delivery velocity from scaling.
Not establishing the CAIO role before restructuring, leaving the three verticals without a single authoritative voice to resolve cross-vertical conflicts.
Treating the target-state as a one-time design rather than an evolving structure — a program with 3 squads today may need 8 squads in two years, requiring planned scale-up protocols.
Staffing agent delivery squads with all-central-team members, bypassing the BU domain expert role — agents that don't incorporate domain knowledge consistently underperform.
Skipping the current-state audit, which means the target-state plan doesn't account for existing skills that can be redeployed vs. new capabilities that must be recruited.
Best practices
Keep delivery squads small (5–8 people) and genuinely cross-functional — a squad of 10 homogeneous engineers is not a squad, it's a team, and lacks the domain-technical balance needed for agent work.
Define explicit interface contracts between the platform team and delivery squads: what platform services exist, how squads request new capabilities, and how platform prioritizes squad requests.
Plan for the BU AI Lead role deliberately: these individuals are rare, need to be identified early, and typically require 6–12 months of development before they're effective in the liaison role.
Use a phased transition that maintains delivery continuity — don't restructure all teams simultaneously, which risks disrupting active projects and losing momentum.
Review the org design annually against the program's scale: the right design for 3 squads is not the right design for 10 squads.
Performance and scalability

The three-vertical design scales delivery capacity almost linearly by adding squads, while platform and governance scale sub-linearly because they serve multiple squads simultaneously. The key constraint is the platform team's capacity to build and maintain shared services: plan for a roughly 1:3 ratio of platform engineers to agent delivery engineers in steady state. When this ratio drops below 1:4, platform becomes a bottleneck and delivery squads begin building their own ad-hoc infrastructure, eroding the value of the shared platform.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool / Framework
Use in Org Design

Team Topologies (book)
Framework for stream-aligned vs. platform vs. enabling teams
Directly applicable: squads = stream-aligned, platform = platform team, CoE = enabling team
OrgDesigner / Orgvue
Organizational design visualization and workforce planning
Useful for gap analysis and transition planning
Workday / SuccessFactors
HR systems to execute headcount plan and track skill inventory
Critical for mapping existing skills to target roles
Comparison with alternatives
Design
Delivery Speed
Governance Quality
Scalability
Three-vertical (target)
High (parallel squads)
High (dedicated CoE)
High (add squads)
Centralized AI team
Medium (queue-constrained)
High (single team)
Low (bottleneck at scale)

Low (inconsistent)
Medium (BU-constrained)
Security and reliability

From a security perspective, the three-vertical design creates natural separation of duties: the platform team controls infrastructure credentials and access controls, the CoE defines security requirements and reviews compliance, and delivery squads operate within the security perimeter established by the platform. No single squad has the ability to both provision infrastructure and deploy to it without oversight — a classic control design for high-assurance systems.

Reliability is supported by the platform team's accountability for shared service uptime. When platform and delivery are merged, reliability often loses to feature velocity. The target-state design makes reliability an explicit responsibility of a dedicated team with SLA commitments to delivery squads, creating institutional accountability for operational excellence.
Key takeaways
Separate platform (build shared infra), governance (set standards), and delivery (build agents) into distinct verticals — each requires a different skill profile and operating rhythm.
The CoE must be a standards and enablement body, not a delivery org — making it a delivery org creates bottlenecks that limit the program's velocity.
Agent delivery squads must be genuinely cross-functional, including domain experts from the business — agents without domain knowledge consistently underperform.
Establish the CAIO role before restructuring the teams — the CAIO is the organizing authority that makes the three-vertical design coherent and conflict-free.
Plan for a phased 12–24 month transition rather than a simultaneous reorganization — delivery continuity during restructuring is critical to maintaining stakeholder trust.
Target a 1:3 platform-to-delivery engineer ratio in steady state; below 1:4, the platform becomes a bottleneck and delivery squads build their own shadow infrastructure.
